what: how is map different from object
why: why use map instead of object
how: how to use object as key
how to iterate over a map

1_beginner_explanation.summary: A **Map** is a specialized data structure similar to an Object: it holds key-value pairs. But it fixes three massive annoyances with normal Objects: 1) Normal Objects ONLY allow strings/symbols as keys; Maps allow *anything* (even other objects) as keys. 2) Normal Objects don't know how big they are; Maps have a built-in `.size` property. 3) Maps are extremely easy to loop over and maintain their exact insertion order.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: A normal Object is like a cheap label maker—it can only print text labels. A Map is like a heavy-duty museum display case. You can use literally anything as the 'label' for a display, even putting a smaller statue (an object) next to a bigger statue to act as its identifier label.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: Maps use a deterministic Hash Table under the hood. When you do `map.set(key, value)`, it hashes the key's memory address (if it's an object) or value (if primitive) to store it in an O(1) accessible slot, while maintaining a parallel linked list to preserve iteration order.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Keys in a standard Map are strongly held in memory. If you use an object as a Map key, that object cannot be Garbage Collected as long as it exists in the Map.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Attaching extra metadata to DOM elements without modifying the DOM element itself.

4_common_confusion.trap: Using standard object syntax `map[key] = value` on a Map. While it won't throw an error, it completely breaks the Map. It just adds a generic object property instead of adding it to the Map's internal hash table.

6_edge_case.quirk: Because Maps check keys by Reference, two identical-looking objects are considered entirely different keys in a Map.
